client_information.story_highlights_briefly_what_you_need_h:
I am requesting assistance with past-due rent and utility bills so that my two children and I can remain in our apartment and avoid eviction. I am a single mother of two (ages 7 and 11) living in the Badger High School District. I work part-time as a housekeeper at a local hotel in Lake Geneva, but my hours were significantly reduced over the past several months due to slow business and staff cutbacks. As a result, I fell behind on rent and some utilities.

Right now, I owe $1,125 in back rent for October and November, plus a $75 late fee, for a total of about $1,200 to my landlord. My landlord has been patient but has now given me a written notice that if I do not pay at least one full month of back rent plus the late fee by the end of this month, he will move forward with eviction paperwork. I am very worried because I do not have family close by who can take us in if we lose our housing, and my children are settled in their school here.

In addition to rent, I am behind on my electric and gas bill with Alliant Energy by about $286.47. I received a disconnection warning stating that services may be shut off if I do not make a payment arrangement and pay at least $150 by early next month. I have applied for Energy Assistance through Walworth County, but they told me it can take several weeks to process, and I have not received a decision yet. I also owe about $93.20 on my city water bill, which is one bill past due.

The main reason I fell behind is that, starting in late summer, my work hours were cut from around 32–35 hours per week down to 18–20 hours. There were multiple weeks where I only got 3 or 4 short shifts. I tried to apply for unemployment to make up the difference, but I had trouble completing the application online because of documentation issues and confusion about how to report my variable hours. I am in the process of reapplying with help from a caseworker, but I have not received any unemployment payments yet. During this time, my son also needed some dental work that cost about $210 out of pocket after insurance, which used up what little savings I had.

I am doing everything I can to get back on track. I picked up a few extra cleaning jobs on weekends for a neighbor and through my church, which brings in maybe an extra $80–$100 a month, and I have been using the local food pantry to reduce grocery costs. I am also actively asking my supervisor for any extra shifts when someone calls off. Even with all of that, I am still short on what I need to catch up on both rent and utilities all at once.

I am asking Side by Side for help specifically with either one month of back rent (approximately $600) or a combination of partial rent and the most urgent utilities (for example, $400 toward rent and $200–$250 toward the Alliant Energy bill), depending on what is possible under your program. Any assistance that can be provided would keep us in our home, prevent our lights and heat from being shut off as winter is starting, and give me a chance to stabilize our situation as I work on getting my hours back up and finishing my unemployment application. I am willing to provide any documentation needed, including the eviction notice, utility bills, pay stubs, and my Energy Assistance application receipt.
client_information.other_resources_to_help_you_out_family_f:
My landlord, James Walker, has been willing to hold off on filing the eviction as long as I keep him updated and make small payments when I can. He allowed me to pay $100 at the beginning of this month instead of the full $600 rent, with the understanding that I am seeking assistance.

I have a sister, Ana, who lives in Milwaukee, but she is a home health aide and is also struggling financially. She was able to lend me $50 in October to help cover part of the electric bill, but she cannot help with larger amounts.

I attend St. Mark’s Lutheran Church in Lake Geneva. The church has given us grocery gift cards totaling $75 over the past two months and connected me with a volunteer who sometimes gives us rides when my car isn’t working. They do not have funds available right now to help with rent, which is why they suggested I contact Side by Side.

I also have a neighbor in my building, Mrs. Patel, who has occasionally watched my kids after school so I can pick up extra cleaning jobs. She does this as a favor and does not charge me, which has helped a lot with childcare costs.
client_information.other_agencies_helping_you_salvation_arm:
I am currently getting help from several places:

- Walworth County Health and Human Services: I have an open case with them. They help me with BadgerCare Plus health insurance for myself and my children. I also receive FoodShare (about $380 per month) to help cover groceries. I met with a caseworker, Ms. Laura Jensen, in October, and she helped me start a new application for unemployment and Energy Assistance.

- Walworth County Energy Assistance Program: I submitted an application on 2025-10-28 for help with my heating and electric bills. I provided copies of my pay stubs from the hotel and my Alliant Energy bill. They told me processing might take 4–6 weeks. I have not been approved or denied yet.

- Local food pantry: I go to the Lake Geneva area food pantry about twice a month. They provide canned goods, pasta, rice, cereal, some fresh produce, and sometimes milk and eggs. This has helped me stretch my FoodShare benefits and free up a little money for bills.

- St. Mark’s Lutheran Church: As mentioned, they have given us grocery gift cards and sometimes put my kids’ names on lists for school supply drives and winter coats. They also referred me to Side by Side and gave me the phone number to call.

I have not received any funds from Salvation Army yet, but I did call their office in November and they said they were low on funds and suggested I try Side by Side as well.
